alsoKnownAs: Half-Banded Spiny Eel (trade misapplication — correctly M. circumcinctus)

nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving to still freshwater: rivers, floodplains, ponds, canals, and marshes with soft muddy or sandy substrates; dense vegetation and abundant detritus cover; low-to-moderate current

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (mandatory — minimum 5 cm deep for burrowing)
smooth-edged caves and PVC pipes as hiding sites
driftwood and leaf litter for cover
dense planted borders around tank edges
dim lighting or floating plants to reduce ambient light

diet.notes: Micro-carnivore specialising in small invertebrates. Prefers live or frozen bloodworms, tubifex, blackworms, brine shrimp, daphnia, and small earthworm pieces. Wild diet comprises benthic worms, insect larvae, and small crustaceans. Newly imported specimens almost always require live foods initially; weaning onto frozen equivalents is possible with patience but may take weeks. Sinking carnivore pellets are sometimes accepted by established individuals. Feed at dusk or after lights-out owing to strict nocturnal feeding behaviour.

goodWith: medium-bodied peaceful tetras (black skirt, bleeding heart)
avoidWith: other spiny eels unless tank is large with ample cover (territorial fights)
boisterous large fish that outcompete it at feeding time

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Mastacembelus pancalus; confirm image resolves before uploading.

summary: A smaller South Asian spiny eel (~18 cm) with a bold zig-zag lateral stripe. Strictly nocturnal and a dedicated burrower; fine sand substrate is mandatory. Less commonly traded than the Tire Track or Fire Eel but hardier and better suited to medium community tanks. Copper-sensitive; tightly lidded tanks essential.
careNotes:
The Zig-Zag Eel (Mastacembelus pancalus) is a compact spiny eel native to the slow-moving and still freshwaters of India, Bangladesh, and Nepal. It is one of the smaller Mastacembelus species, reaching around 15–18 cm in aquaria, making it more manageable than its popular relatives the Tire Track Eel (M. armatus, fw-fish-133) and Fire Eel (M. erythrotaenia, fw-fish-182). Despite its modest size, it shares the same demanding husbandry requirements common to the family.

Substrate is the single most critical parameter: a deep (≥5 cm) bed of fine, smooth sand is mandatory. This species burrows extensively both to rest and to feel secure; coarse gravel or bare-bottom tanks cause chronic stress and abrasion injuries to the delicate underbelly. Rounded river sand or pool-filter sand are preferred over sharp silica.

The species is strictly nocturnal. Food should be offered after lights-out or at dusk; daytime feeding attempts are usually ignored. Live or frozen bloodworms, blackworms, tubifex, and brine shrimp are the staple diet. Newly imported animals almost invariably refuse dry foods; live worms are generally the only reliable way to start feeding. With time, most individuals can be weaned to frozen bloodworm or high-protein sinking pellets, but this transition requires patience.

Escape risk is high. Mastacembelids are notorious escape artists capable of fitting through very small gaps; a tightly sealed lid with no open filter slots is non-negotiable. Even a small gap at the water surface will be exploited.

Copper-based medications (common treatments for ich and other parasites) are lethal to this species. Use only copper-free alternatives such as heat treatment or formalin-based products, and never dose a tank housing this fish with copper compounds.

Note on trade confusion: \"Zig-Zag Eel\" is the dominant trade name for M. pancalus, but the same common name is occasionally misapplied to M. circumcinctus (Half-Banded Spiny Eel, fw-fish-370). M. circumcinctus has a different banding pattern and distribution; confirm the scientific name when purchasing. M. pancalus can be distinguished by its continuous zig-zag lateral line marking rather than the broken half-bands of M. circumcinctus.

Water quality must be excellent — spiny eels are sensitive to elevated ammonia and nitrite. Weekly partial water changes of 25–30% are recommended. The species tolerates a moderately wide temperature range (22–28°C) but should be kept toward the warmer end for feeding activity.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of M. pancalus has been reported occasionally but is uncommon in the hobby. Like other mastacembelids, spawning is triggered by simulating the onset of the monsoon season with warmer water (up to 28–30°C), increased water changes with slightly cooler fresh water, and reduced photoperiod followed by a gradual return to normal. Eggs are typically scattered among fine-leaved plants or deposited near the substrate. Parents do not guard eggs and will consume them if not removed. Fry are tiny and require infusoria or newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii as first foods. Successful captive reproduction in a community tank is unlikely; a dedicated breeding setup is required.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Mastacembelus pancalus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size, temperature range (22–28°C), pH (6.5–7.5), hardness, native range (India, Bangladesh, Nepal), habitat (slow/still waters with soft substrates), diet (worms, insect larvae, small crustaceans), nocturnal behaviour, burrowing requirements, and breeding notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Mastacembelus pancalus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (up to 18 cm TL), native countries (India, Bangladesh, Nepal), taxonomy (Mastacembelidae, Synbranchiformes), and temperature tolerance. Habitat listed as demersal freshwater.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Spiny Eel Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-focused care overview covering the Mastacembelidae family; confirmed copper sensitivity, high escape risk, mandatory sand substrate, nocturnal feeding behaviour, and live/frozen food preference. Noted M. pancalus as one of the smaller and more manageable hobby species.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Spiny Eels
additional.notes: UK trade and husbandry overview; confirmed copper sensitivity across Mastacembelidae, fine sand substrate requirement, escape artist behaviour necessitating tight lids, and nocturnal carnivore feeding notes. Referenced M. pancalus as traded under the zig-zag eel name in the UK market.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Spiny Eels in the Aquarium
additional.notes: Hobbyist-level species overview; confirmed burrowing behaviour, minimum sand depth recommendations, reluctance to accept dry foods on arrival, and note distinguishing M. pancalus from M. circumcinctus (Half-Banded Spiny Eel) in trade.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding rarely achieved; requires monsoon-simulation trigger (temperature ramp, cooler water changes), fine-leaved plants for egg deposition, and immediate removal of parents. Fry need infusoria or nauplii. Not regularly bred in captivity.
